sections.title: Shell & environment
sections.subtitle: Customise your shell, set variables, and make Linux yours

lessons.title: Shell variables
lessons.description: Create, read, and use variables in the shell

steps.label: Creating and reading variables
steps.text:
Shell variables store values you can reference later.

<cmd>NAME=value</cmd>        — set a variable (<warn>no spaces around =</warn>)
<cmd>echo $NAME</cmd>        — read the variable (note the <hl>$</hl> prefix)
<cmd>echo "Hello $NAME"</cmd> — use it inside a string
<cmd>unset NAME</cmd>        — delete the variable

Variable names are case-sensitive. By convention:
<hl>UPPER_CASE</hl> — environment variables (system-wide)
<hl>lower_case</hl> — local script variables

Variables set this way only exist in the current shell session.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: Which of these correctly sets a shell variable?

feedback.ok: Correct! No spaces around the = sign. NAME=Alice is the correct syntax.
feedback.bad: Spaces around = cause an error in bash. The correct syntax is NAME=Alice with no spaces.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To read the value of a variable called PORT, use echo

feedback.ok: Correct! $ tells the shell to expand the variable: echo $PORT
feedback.bad: $ is the prefix to read a variable's value: echo $PORT

steps.label: Scope
steps.question: You set MYVAR=hello in one terminal window. You open a second terminal. Is MYVAR available there?
options: No — shell variables only exist in the current shell session

feedback.ok: Correct! Shell variables are local to the session. To share across sessions, use export.
feedback.bad: Shell variables are local to the current shell process. You need export to make them available to child processes.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Create a variable called GREETING with the value 'Hello Linux' then print it.
steps.hint: Set the variable, then use echo $GREETING to print it. Use quotes if the value has spaces.

lessons.title: Environment variables — export & env
lessons.description: Make variables available to child processes and view the environment

steps.label: export and env
steps.text:
<hl>Environment variables</hl> are variables that are passed to all child processes.

<cmd>export VAR=value</cmd>   — set and export a variable
<cmd>export VAR</cmd>         — export an already-set variable
<cmd>env</cmd>                — list all current environment variables
<cmd>printenv VAR</cmd>       — print the value of one variable
<cmd>unset VAR</cmd>          — remove a variable

Common environment variables:
<hl>HOME</hl>   — your home directory (/home/user)
<hl>USER</hl>   — your username
<hl>SHELL</hl>  — your current shell (/bin/bash)
<hl>LANG</hl>   — your locale setting

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What is the difference between a shell variable and an environment variable?
options: Shell variables use uppercase, environment variables use lowercase
Environment variables (exported) are passed to child processes; shell variables are not
Environment variables are stored on disk

feedback.ok: Correct! export makes a variable part of the environment, visible to any child process started from this shell.
feedback.bad: Shell variables stay in the current shell. export promotes them to environment variables, which are inherited by child processes.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To see the value of the HOME environment variable, run

feedback.ok: Correct! printenv HOME shows the value of the HOME variable.
feedback.bad: printenv NAME prints a single environment variable's value. Or use echo $HOME.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Export an environment variable called API_KEY with value 'abc123':

feedback.ok: Correct! export comes first, then the variable assignment.
feedback.bad: export VAR=value — the export keyword comes first, then the variable name and value.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.hint: Use the env command to list all environment variables.

output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: env (lists all environment variables)</info>

lessons.title: The PATH variable
lessons.description: Understand how Linux finds commands and customise your PATH

steps.label: How PATH works
steps.text:
<hl>PATH</hl> is an environment variable that tells the shell <hl>where to look</hl> for commands.

It's a colon-separated list of directories:
<cmd>echo $PATH</cmd>
<hl>/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/home/user/.local/bin</hl>

When you type <cmd>git</cmd>, the shell searches each directory in PATH from left to right until it finds an executable named 'git'. If it finds none, you get 'command not found'.

To add a directory to PATH:
<cmd>export PATH="$HOME/scripts:$PATH"</cmd>

Always append <hl>$PATH</hl> at the end to keep the existing directories — replacing PATH entirely would break all commands.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You type a command but get 'command not found'. What is the most likely reason?
options: The command's directory is not in your PATH

feedback.ok: Correct! 'command not found' usually means the executable exists but its directory isn't in PATH.
feedback.bad: When the shell can't find a command, it's usually because the directory containing it isn't listed in PATH.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To safely add ~/bin to your PATH without removing existing directories, run: export PATH="~/bin:

feedback.ok: Correct! Always include $PATH when extending it, otherwise you lose all existing command locations.
feedback.bad: $PATH preserves the existing path. Without it, you'd overwrite PATH and lose access to all commands.

steps.label: which command
steps.question: What does 'which python3' tell you?
options: The full path to the python3 executable that will be run

feedback.ok: Correct! which searches PATH and prints the full path of the first matching executable.
feedback.bad: which shows you exactly which file will be run when you type that command — useful when multiple versions exist.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.hint: Use echo with the $ prefix to read the PATH variable.

output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: echo $PATH or printenv PATH</info>

lessons.title: Config files — .bashrc & .profile
lessons.description: Make your customisations permanent by editing shell config files

steps.label: bashrc and profile
steps.text:
Changes you make in the terminal disappear when you close it. To make them permanent, add them to config files:

<hl>~/.bashrc</hl>     — runs every time you open a new interactive terminal
<hl>~/.profile</hl>    — runs on login (once per session)
<hl>~/.bash_profile</hl> — login shell config (overrides .profile if present)

To apply changes without opening a new terminal:
<cmd>source ~/.bashrc</cmd>   — reload the file
<cmd>. ~/.bashrc</cmd>        — shorthand for source

Put these in ~/.bashrc:
  • Aliases
  • PATH additions
  • Environment variable exports
  • Custom prompt (PS1)

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You add export MY_VAR=hello to ~/.bashrc. When does this take effect?
options: After you run source ~/.bashrc or open a new terminal
Only in scripts, not interactive sessions

feedback.ok: Correct! You must source the file or open a new terminal for changes to apply.
feedback.bad: Editing ~/.bashrc doesn't change the current environment. Run source ~/.bashrc or open a new terminal.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To reload ~/.bashrc in your current terminal without closing it, run:

feedback.ok: Correct! source (or its shorthand .) executes the file in the current shell.
feedback.bad: source ~/.bashrc (or . ~/.bashrc) re-runs the config file in the current shell session.

steps.label: Which file?
steps.question: Where should you add a PATH update so it applies every time you open a terminal?

feedback.ok: Correct! ~/.bashrc is sourced every time you open a new interactive bash shell.
feedback.bad: ~/.bashrc is the right place — it's read every time you open a new terminal window.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Reload your .bashrc file to apply recent changes.
steps.hint: Use source followed by the path to .bashrc.

output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Try: source ~/.bashrc
The dot shorthand also works: . ~/.bashrc</info>

lessons.title: Aliases
lessons.description: Create shortcuts for long or frequently used commands

steps.label: Creating aliases
steps.text:
<hl>Aliases</hl> let you create shorthand names for commands you use often.

<cmd>alias ll='ls -la'</cmd>          — create an alias
<cmd>alias gs='git status'</cmd>      — git shortcut
<cmd>alias ..='cd ..'</cmd>           — navigate faster
<cmd>alias</cmd>                      — list all defined aliases
<cmd>unalias ll</cmd>                 — remove an alias

Aliases set in the terminal last only for the session. To make them permanent, add them to <hl>~/.bashrc</hl>:

<cmd>echo "alias ll='ls -la'" >> ~/.bashrc</cmd>

terminal.output:
total 48K
drwxr-xr-x  5 user staff 4.0K Mar 10 ./
drwxr-xr-x 12 root root  4.0K Mar  8 ../
-rw-r--r--  1 user staff 3.2K Mar 10 .bashrc
-rw-r--r--  1 user staff  512 Mar 10 notes.txt

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You define alias gs='git status'. You open a new terminal. Does gs still work?
options: No — session aliases disappear when the terminal closes
Only if you ran it as sudo

feedback.ok: Correct! Session aliases disappear when the terminal closes. Add them to ~/.bashrc to make them permanent.
feedback.bad: Aliases set in the terminal are temporary. To persist them, add the alias command to ~/.bashrc.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To make the alias ll='ls -la' permanent, add it to ~/.bashrc using: echo "alias ll='ls -la'"

feedback.ok: Correct! >> appends to the file without overwriting it. > would delete the whole file first!
feedback.bad: >> appends to a file. > would overwrite (delete) the entire .bashrc first — very dangerous!

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Create an alias 'update' for 'sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade':

feedback.ok: Correct! alias name='command' is the format.
feedback.bad: Format: alias name='command'. The name and = must be together with no spaces.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.hint: Run the alias command with no arguments to list all aliases.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>alias ll='ls -la'
alias gs='git status'
alias ..='cd ..'
alias grep='grep --color=auto'</info>
output.bad:
<e>✗ Not quite.</e>
<info>Just run: alias (no arguments) to list all defined aliases.</info>

lessons.title: Command history
lessons.description: Navigate and reuse your command history efficiently

steps.label: Shell history
steps.text:
Bash saves every command you type in a history file (<hl>~/.bash_history</hl>).

<cmd>history</cmd>         — show numbered history list
<cmd>history 20</cmd>      — show last 20 commands
<cmd>!42</cmd>             — re-run command number 42
<cmd>!!</cmd>              — repeat the last command
<cmd>!git</cmd>            — repeat the last command starting with 'git'
<cmd>Ctrl+R</cmd>          — interactive reverse search (most powerful)

Ctrl+R opens an incremental search — type part of a past command and press Ctrl+R again to cycle through matches. Press Enter to run, Esc to cancel.

terminal.output:
196  ls -la
  197  cd projects
  198  git status
  199  cat README.md
  200  history 5

On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean

feedback.ok: Correct! !! is shorthand for 'repeat the previous command'. Very useful for: sudo !!
feedback.bad: !! repeats the last command. A common use: you forget sudo, then type sudo !! to rerun with sudo.

steps.label: History search
steps.question: What keyboard shortcut opens the interactive reverse history search in bash?

feedback.ok: Correct! Ctrl+R opens reverse-i-search. Start typing and it finds matching past commands instantly.
feedback.bad: Ctrl+R opens the reverse incremental search. It's the fastest way to find a command you used earlier.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: Bash stores your command history in a file called

feedback.ok: Correct! ~/.bash_history is the default history file. It's written when you exit the shell.
feedback.bad: ~/.bash_history stores your command history. It's written when the shell exits.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Show the last 10 commands from your history.
steps.hint: Use history followed by the number of lines you want, or pipe it to tail.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>  191  git pull
  192  npm install
  193  npm run dev
  194  ls -la
  195  cat .env
  196  git status
  197  cd ..
  198  ls
  199  pwd
  200  history 10</info>

lessons.title: Section 6 review
lessons.description: Test your understanding of the shell environment

steps.label: Review
steps.question: You set NAME=Linux in your terminal. Your friend opens a new terminal. Can they echo $NAME?
options: No — shell variables are local to the session
Only if they log in as the same user

feedback.ok: Correct! Shell variables are session-local. Use export and add to ~/.bashrc to share across sessions.
feedback.bad: Shell variables only exist in the current process. A new terminal gets a fresh environment.

steps.label: Review
sentence: To make a variable called DB_URL available to programs you launch from the shell, use:

feedback.ok: Correct! export makes the variable part of the environment, available to child processes.
feedback.bad: export promotes a shell variable to an environment variable, making it available to child processes.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: What happens if you set PATH=~/bin (without $PATH at the end)?
options: ~/bin is safely added to the existing path
PATH is overwritten — all other commands will stop working
Only temporary — PATH resets on next command

feedback.ok: Correct! Overwriting PATH without $PATH destroys the existing paths. Always use: export PATH="~/bin:$PATH"
feedback.bad: Overwriting PATH replaces all existing entries. You'd lose access to ls, cd, and every other command. Always append: $PATH at the end.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: What is the fastest way to search for a command you used two days ago?
options: Ctrl+R and start typing part of the command

feedback.ok: Correct! Ctrl+R opens an interactive reverse search — just type a few characters and it finds matches instantly.
feedback.bad: Ctrl+R is the fastest — it opens an interactive incremental search through your history as you type.

steps.label: Review
sentence: To make an alias permanent, add it to ~/.bashrc and then run

feedback.ok: Correct! source reloads the file in the current shell without opening a new terminal.
feedback.bad: source ~/.bashrc (or . ~/.bashrc) applies changes immediately without needing a new terminal.

steps.label: Review
steps.question: !! is typed after a failed command. What does it do?
options: Deletes the last command from history

feedback.ok: Correct! !! reruns the last command. A famous use: you forgot sudo, then type sudo !! to rerun with sudo.
feedback.bad: !! is shorthand for 'repeat the last command'. Commonly used as sudo !! to rerun with elevated privileges.
